title: Daily Standup Básico
description: Aprenda a estrutura clássica de uma daily standup

5.text:
Good morning everyone. Let's start our daily standup.

I'll begin with my updates. Yesterday, I finished the project documentation and sent it to the team for review. Today, I'm working on implementing the new search feature. I'm planning to have the first draft ready by end of day.

I don't have any blockers at the moment. That's all from my end.

10.text:
Good morning everyone. I hope you all had a great weekend.

Let's dive into our daily standup. I'll kick things off with my updates.

Yesterday was quite productive. I wrapped up the project documentation and circulated it to the team for review. I'm hoping to get everyone's feedback by Wednesday so we can incorporate changes before the stakeholder meeting.

I also had a really productive pairing session with Sarah yesterday afternoon. We knocked out the authentication module together.

Today, I'm focusing on implementing the new search feature. The plan is to have a working prototype by end of day, which should give us enough time to run it through QA tomorrow.

In terms of blockers, I'm in pretty good shape right now. However, I anticipate I might need some guidance from the design team this afternoon regarding the UI layout. That's all from my end. Over to you, Mike!

15.text:
Good morning everyone. I hope you all had a great weekend. Quick question before we start - did anyone get a chance to look at the architecture proposal I shared on Friday?

Anyway, let's dive into our daily standup. I'll kick things off with my updates.

So yesterday was quite productive. I wrapped up the project documentation and circulated it to the team for review. I'm hoping to get everyone's feedback by Wednesday so we can incorporate changes before the stakeholder meeting on Thursday. The documentation covers all the technical specifications, user stories, and acceptance criteria.

I also had a really productive pairing session with Sarah yesterday afternoon. We knocked out the authentication module together, and I have to say, her approach to handling edge cases was brilliant. We should probably share that pattern with the rest of the team. We managed to reduce the code complexity significantly while improving security.

Moving forward, today I'm focusing on implementing the new search feature. The plan is to have a working prototype by end of day, which should give us enough time to run it through QA tomorrow and make any necessary tweaks before Friday's demo. I'm using Elasticsearch for the backend, which should provide fast and accurate results.

In terms of blockers, I'm in pretty good shape right now. However, I anticipate I might need some guidance from the design team this afternoon regarding the UI layout for the search results page. I know they're swamped, so I'll try to be as specific as possible with my questions to respect their time.

One more thing - I noticed we're running low on API rate limits. Might be worth discussing if we should upgrade our plan or optimize our calls. Just putting it on everyone's radar.

That's all from my end. Over to you, Mike!
culturalTips: Americanos valorizam brevidade mas também contexto
Reconhecer contribuições de colegas é bem visto

title: Daily com Bloqueadores
description: Comunique problemas e peça ajuda de forma profissional

5.text:
Good morning team. Let me share my updates.

Yesterday I made progress on the payment integration, but I've hit a blocker. The API documentation is unclear about authentication headers, and I'm getting 401 errors.

Could someone from the backend team help me debug this? I'd really appreciate it.

Today I'll focus on the UI components while waiting for help with the API issue.

10.text:
Good morning team. Thanks for jumping on the call.

So yesterday I made solid progress on the payment integration. I got the frontend form working perfectly, and users can now enter their credit card information smoothly. However, I've hit a significant blocker on the backend side.

The issue is with the payment gateway API. The documentation is unclear about which authentication headers are required, and I'm consistently getting 401 unauthorized errors. I've tried several approaches - Basic Auth, Bearer tokens, API keys in different formats - but nothing seems to work.

I've already reached out to the payment provider's support team, but they usually take 24-48 hours to respond. In the meantime, could someone from our backend team help me debug this? I'd really appreciate a second pair of eyes on this.

For today, I'll pivot to work on the UI components for the checkout confirmation page while waiting for help with the API issue. That way I can still make progress without being completely blocked.

Does anyone have experience with this payment gateway? Any suggestions would be helpful!

15.text:
Good morning team. Thanks for jumping on the call. I really appreciate everyone's time.

Let me walk you through where I'm at with the payment integration project. Yesterday was a mix of progress and challenges, so bear with me.

On the positive side, I made solid progress on the frontend. The payment form is now fully functional - users can enter their credit card information, the validation is working beautifully, and the UX feels smooth. I also added proper error handling for edge cases like expired cards and invalid CVV codes.

However, I've hit a significant blocker on the backend integration that's preventing me from moving forward. The issue is with the payment gateway API. Their documentation is surprisingly unclear about authentication requirements. I'm consistently getting 401 unauthorized errors when trying to process test transactions.

Here's what I've tried so far: Basic Auth with the API credentials, Bearer tokens in the Authorization header, and API keys in various formats - query parameters, custom headers, and even in the request body. Nothing seems to work. I've double-checked that my credentials are correct by logging into their dashboard successfully.

I've already reached out to the payment provider's support team yesterday afternoon, but based on their SLA, they usually take 24-48 hours to respond. I also searched their community forum and Stack Overflow, but the few similar issues I found don't have clear solutions.

So here's where I need help: Could someone from our backend team help me debug this? I'd really appreciate a second pair of eyes on the authentication flow. Maybe I'm missing something obvious? I can share my screen and walk you through the code after this call.

In terms of moving forward, I've created a backup plan. For today, I'll pivot to work on the UI components for the checkout confirmation page and the success/failure screens. That way I can still make progress without being completely blocked. I estimate that's about 4-5 hours of work, which should keep me busy while we resolve the API issue.

Does anyone have experience with this particular payment gateway? Or any suggestions on what else I could try? I'm also open to the idea of switching to a different provider if this becomes too much of a time sink, though I know that would delay the project.

That's where I'm at. Happy to answer any questions!
culturalTips: Ser transparente sobre problemas é valorizado
Mostrar que você tentou resolver sozinho primeiro
Ter backup plan mostra maturidade profissional

5.text:
Hi Sarah, thanks for making time for our 1:1.

I wanted to discuss my career growth. I've been in this role for about a year now, and I'm interested in taking on more responsibility.

Specifically, I'd like to work on more complex projects and maybe mentor junior developers. What do you think would be a good next step for me?

10.text:
Hi Sarah, thanks for making time for our 1:1 today. I really appreciate these check-ins.

I wanted to use our time today to discuss my career development. I've been in my current role for about a year now, and I feel like I've grown a lot. I'm consistently delivering projects on time, my code reviews have been positive, and I've been getting good feedback from the team.

Given that progress, I'm interested in taking on more responsibility and challenges. Specifically, I'd like to work on more complex, high-impact projects. I'm also interested in developing my leadership skills - maybe by mentoring junior developers or leading a small project team.

I wanted to get your perspective on this. What do you think would be a good next step for me? Are there any areas where you think I should focus on improving first?

Also, I'd love to hear your honest feedback on my performance. What am I doing well, and where do you see room for growth?

15.text:
Hi Sarah, thanks so much for making time for our 1:1 today. I know your schedule has been crazy lately, so I really appreciate you prioritizing this.

I wanted to use our time today to have a conversation about my career development and get your input on some ideas I've been thinking about.

First, let me share where I feel I'm at professionally. I've been in my current role for about a year now, and I genuinely feel like I've grown a lot during this time. I'm consistently delivering projects on time and within scope, my code reviews have been overwhelmingly positive, and I've been getting really encouraging feedback from teammates about my collaboration and communication skills.

That said, I'm starting to feel like I'm ready for new challenges. Don't get me wrong - I love what I'm doing, but I think I've reached a point where I'm comfortable with my current responsibilities, and I'm eager to stretch myself further.

Specifically, I'm interested in taking on more responsibility in a couple of areas. First, I'd love to work on more complex, high-impact projects - the kind that really move the needle for the business. I think I've proven I can handle the technical challenges, and I'm excited to tackle bigger problems.

Second, I'm really interested in developing my leadership skills. I've been thinking it might be valuable for me to mentor some of our junior developers. I remember how helpful my early mentors were to me, and I'd love to pay that forward. Plus, I think explaining concepts to others would strengthen my own understanding.

I've also been wondering if there might be opportunities to lead a small project team in the future. Nothing massive to start - maybe a 2-3 person team on a shorter initiative - just to get some experience with the coordination and people management side of things.

So I wanted to get your perspective on all of this. What do you think would be a good next step for me? Are these realistic goals given where I am in my career? And are there any areas where you think I should focus on improving or gaining more experience before taking on these kinds of responsibilities?

Also, I'd really value your honest feedback on my overall performance. What do you see as my biggest strengths? And where do you see the most significant room for growth? I want to make sure I'm being self-aware about my abilities and my gaps.

Finally, is there anything specific you think I should be working on or demonstrating to position myself for that next level? I'm committed to putting in the work - I just want to make sure I'm focused on the right things.

I know that's a lot, so please feel free to be candid. I can handle constructive criticism - it's actually what I'm looking for!
keywords: committed to putting in the work
culturalTips: Pedir feedback direto é visto positivamente
Ser específico sobre objetivos ajuda o manager a te apoiar

5.text:
Thanks everyone for joining our sprint retrospective.

What went well: We shipped all committed features on time. The team collaboration was great.

What didn't go well: We had too many interruptions from urgent bugs. Our estimation was off on the authentication feature.

Action items: Let's schedule a dedicated bug fixing day next sprint and spend more time on estimation.

10.text:
Thanks everyone for joining our sprint retrospective. Let's reflect on the last two weeks.

Starting with what went well: First, we successfully shipped all committed features on time, which is a big win. The team collaboration was excellent - I particularly appreciated how quickly everyone responded to questions in Slack. Also, our code review process felt smoother this sprint.

Now, what didn't go well: We had too many interruptions from production bugs. About 30% of our time went to firefighting instead of planned work. Our estimation was also way off on the authentication feature - we estimated 3 days but it took a full week. That threw off our sprint planning.

For action items: I propose we schedule a dedicated bug fixing day at the start of each sprint to reduce interruptions. We should also spend more time on estimation, maybe using planning poker. Finally, let's document the authentication patterns we learned for future reference.

What does everyone think?

15.text:
Thanks everyone for joining our sprint retrospective. I know it's been a long two weeks, so I appreciate everyone taking the time to reflect with the team.

Let me structure this in our usual format: what went well, what didn't go well, and action items for improvement.

Starting with the positives - what went well this sprint. First and foremost, we successfully shipped all our committed features on time, which is a significant achievement given the scope. That's three major features and five smaller improvements. The team collaboration was excellent throughout - I particularly appreciated how quickly everyone responded to questions in Slack, even when working across time zones. Our daily standups were concise but informative. Also, our code review process felt noticeably smoother this sprint. The average PR review time dropped from 8 hours to about 3 hours, which really helped maintain our momentum.

Another positive: The pair programming sessions we tried this sprint were really valuable. Working with Jennifer on the database optimization was a great learning experience, and I think we delivered better code as a result.

Now, let's talk about what didn't go well and could be improved. The biggest issue was interruptions from production bugs. Looking at the data, about 30% of our total sprint capacity went to firefighting urgent issues instead of planned work. That's roughly 60 hours across the team. These weren't small bugs either - we had two critical issues that required immediate attention and pulled multiple people away from their sprint commitments.

Another challenge was our estimation accuracy. Our estimation was way off on the authentication feature. We estimated 3 story points, thinking it would take about 3 days, but it ended up taking a full week. That completely threw off our sprint planning and put pressure on the team to catch up. In hindsight, we underestimated the complexity of integrating with the third-party auth provider.

Communication could also be better. There were a few times when important decisions were made in side conversations, and not everyone was aware of the changes. This led to some rework and confusion.

So, for action items moving forward, I have a few proposals. First, regarding the production bugs: I suggest we schedule a dedicated bug fixing day at the start of each sprint - maybe every Monday. This gives us a chance to proactively address issues before they become urgent. We could rotate who's on bug duty to distribute the load fairly.

Second, about estimation: We should invest more time in our planning sessions. Maybe we could try planning poker to get input from everyone, not just the person who'll work on the task. Also, when we're dealing with new technologies or third-party integrations, let's automatically add a buffer for unknowns.

Third, let's create better documentation. We learned a lot about authentication patterns during this sprint - let's document those learnings so future projects can benefit. I can volunteer to start a wiki page if others can contribute their insights.

Finally, for communication: Let's commit to documenting all significant decisions in our project channel, even if they were discussed elsewhere first. This creates a record everyone can reference.

Those are my thoughts, but this is a team retrospective. What does everyone think? Do these action items resonate? Are there other issues we should address? I'm particularly interested in hearing from those who haven't spoken yet.
culturalTips: Retrospectivas são momentos de aprendizado, não de culpa
Focar em processos, não em pessoas
Propostas devem ser específicas e mensuráveis

5.text:
Hi everyone, my name is Maria Silva and I'm a Software Engineer at TechCorp.

I've been with the company for about two years, working mainly on backend development. Before that, I worked at a startup in São Paulo for three years.

I have a degree in Computer Science, and I'm passionate about building scalable systems. In my free time, I enjoy reading tech blogs and contributing to open source projects.

I'm excited to be here and looking forward to working with all of you!

10.text:
Hi everyone, my name is Maria Silva and I'm a Software Engineer here at TechCorp. Thanks for having me today.

Let me tell you a bit about my background. I've been with TechCorp for about two years now, working primarily on backend development for our e-commerce platform. My main focus has been on API development, database optimization, and microservices architecture.

Before joining TechCorp, I spent three years at a fintech startup in São Paulo called PayFast. There, I was part of the founding engineering team and helped build the payment processing system from scratch. It was an incredible learning experience, though quite challenging at times.

I have a Bachelor's degree in Computer Science from USP. During my studies, I developed a strong interest in distributed systems and cloud architecture, which is what led me to my current career path.

Outside of work, I'm really passionate about the tech community. I regularly read tech blogs to stay updated on industry trends, and I try to contribute to open source projects when I have time. I'm also learning about machine learning and AI - it's fascinating how rapidly that field is evolving.

On a more personal note, I love traveling and trying new cuisines. I'm also an amateur photographer - I enjoy capturing landscapes during my trips.

I'm really excited to be part of this team and I'm looking forward to working with all of you. Please feel free to reach out if you want to chat about technology, grab coffee, or if you have any questions. Thanks again!

15.text:
Hi everyone, my name is Maria Silva and I'm a Software Engineer here at TechCorp. First of all, thanks so much for having me today and for this warm welcome. I'm really excited to be joining this team.

Let me take a few minutes to tell you about my background and journey to TechCorp. I've been with the company for about two years now, and I currently work on the backend engineering team for our e-commerce platform. My day-to-day work involves API development, database optimization, and designing microservices architecture. Recently, I've been leading a project to migrate our monolithic application to a microservices architecture, which has been both challenging and incredibly rewarding.

Before joining TechCorp, I spent three years at a fintech startup in São Paulo called PayFast. I joined them as their fifth employee when they were just getting started, so I was part of the founding engineering team. My main responsibility there was building the payment processing system from the ground up. We started with just an MVP and scaled it to process millions of transactions per month. It was an incredible learning experience - I wore many hats, from writing code to making architectural decisions to occasionally handling customer support. The startup world is intense, but I learned more in those three years than I could have imagined.

Going back a bit further in my journey, I have a Bachelor's degree in Computer Science from the University of São Paulo - USP. During my time there, I developed a strong interest in distributed systems and cloud architecture. I did my thesis on database replication strategies, which actually helped me land my first job. My academic background gave me a solid theoretical foundation, but I'll be honest - most of what I know I learned on the job, dealing with real-world problems.

Outside of work, I'm really passionate about the broader tech community and continuous learning. I regularly read tech blogs and follow industry thought leaders to stay updated on trends. I'm particularly interested in how AI is transforming software development. I also try to give back to the community by contributing to open source projects - mainly developer tools and libraries related to Node.js and Python. I find that contributing to open source not only helps others but also improves my own coding skills.

I'm currently learning about machine learning and artificial intelligence in my spare time. I know I'm late to the party, but better late than never, right? I'm taking an online course on deep learning and trying to apply those concepts to small side projects. It's fascinating how rapidly that field is evolving, and I want to make sure I'm not left behind.

On a more personal note, I love traveling and experiencing different cultures. I've been fortunate to visit about fifteen countries so far, and each trip teaches me something new - not just about the world, but also about myself. I'm also really into photography, though I'm definitely still an amateur. I enjoy capturing landscapes and street scenes during my travels. There's something meditative about framing a shot and waiting for the perfect moment.

I also try to maintain a healthy work-life balance. I practice yoga twice a week and go hiking on weekends when the weather is nice. I've learned that taking care of my physical and mental health makes me a better engineer.

So that's me in a nutshell! I know that was a lot of information, but I wanted to give you a sense of who I am both professionally and personally. I'm really excited to be part of this team and to learn from all of you. Everyone I've met so far has been incredibly welcoming and knowledgeable.

I'm always happy to chat about technology, career paths, travel recommendations, or really anything else. My desk is over in the corner by the window, or you can always reach me on Slack. I'm also always up for grabbing coffee or lunch - I'm still exploring the neighborhood and would love recommendations for good spots.

Thanks again for the warm welcome. I'm looking forward to working with all of you and contributing to our projects. If anyone has questions for me, I'm happy to answer them now, or feel free to catch me later!
keywords: more than I could have imagined
a sense of who I am

title: Small Talk no Coffee Break
description: Converse informalmente com colegas de trabalho

5.text:
Hey! How's it going?

Good, thanks! Just grabbing some coffee. How about you?

Pretty good. Busy week, but managing. Did you watch the game last night?

No, I missed it. Who won?

The Lakers! It was a close one. Anyway, how's your project going?

It's going well. We're launching next week, so pretty excited about that.

That's awesome! Good luck with the launch.

Thanks! Have a great day!

10.text:
Hey! How's it going?

Oh hey! Good, thanks! Just taking a quick break and grabbing some coffee. You?

Pretty good, thanks. It's been a busy week, but I'm managing. Counting down to the weekend!

Oh, I hear you. Same here. Got any plans for the weekend?

Actually yes! My family is coming to visit, so we're planning to explore the city a bit. Show them around, you know.

That sounds nice! Where are you taking them?

Thinking of hitting up the waterfront, maybe that new Italian restaurant everyone's been talking about. Have you been there?

No, not yet, but I've heard great things! You'll have to let me know how it is.

Definitely will! How about you? Any weekend plans?

Nothing too exciting. Probably just catching up on some Netflix shows and maybe going for a hike if the weather's nice.

That sounds pretty nice actually. Sometimes a chill weekend is exactly what you need.

Exactly! Okay, I should probably head back. Good chatting with you!

Yeah, you too! Enjoy your weekend with your family!

15.text:
Hey Maria! How's it going?

Oh hey John! Good to see you! I'm doing well, thanks. Just taking a quick break from that meeting marathon I've been in all morning. Grabbing some much-needed coffee. How about you?

I totally feel you on the meeting marathon! Yeah, I'm good. Just finished a pretty intense code review session. My brain needed a break, you know?

Oh man, I know exactly what you mean. Sometimes you just need to step away from the screen for a bit. Code reviews can be draining, especially the long ones.

For sure! Anyway, how's everything going with that big project you were working on? The e-commerce thing?

Oh, it's going pretty well actually! We're in the final stretch now. Planning to launch next week, which is both exciting and terrifying at the same time, if I'm being honest.

That's amazing! I bet you're ready to get it out there after all this work. What's the scariest part?

Honestly? The scale. This is the first time I'm launching something that's going to be used by hundreds of thousands of people from day one. There's this constant worry like 'what if something breaks?' But I guess that's part of the job, right?

Absolutely. But from what I've seen of your work, you guys have been super thorough. I'm sure it'll go smoothly. And hey, if something does break, you'll fix it. That's what we do!

Thanks, I really appreciate that. Sometimes you just need to hear that, you know? Anyway, enough about work! Got any plans for the weekend?

Actually, yeah! My parents and my sister are flying in on Friday. They've never been to this city before, so I'm playing tour guide for a few days.

Oh that's so nice! That must be exciting. Where are you planning to take them?

Well, I'm thinking of hitting up the waterfront on Saturday - showing them the pier, maybe do that boat tour thing. And then on Sunday, we're checking out that new Italian restaurant everyone's been raving about. You know, the one that just opened downtown?

Oh, Bella Notte? Yeah, I've heard amazing things about that place! The pasta is supposed to be incredible. You'll have to let me know how it is - I've been wanting to try it.

Will do! I'm pretty excited about it. My mom's Italian, so she's got high standards when it comes to Italian food. This could either go really well or be very disappointing!

Haha, no pressure then! I'm sure it'll be great though. How long are they staying?

Just until Monday evening. It's a short visit, but it'll be nice to have them here. I haven't seen them since the holidays.

Aww, that's sweet. Family time is important, especially when you don't get to see them often. I hope you all have a great time!

Thanks! What about you? Got anything exciting planned, or just a chill weekend?

Nothing too exciting for me. I'm actually looking forward to a pretty low-key weekend. Maybe catch up on some of those Netflix shows I've been meaning to watch. You know, The Bear? Everyone keeps telling me I have to watch it.

Oh dude, yes! You absolutely need to watch it. It's so good! I binged the whole thing in like two days.

That's what I keep hearing! Okay, you've convinced me. That's definitely happening this weekend. Maybe I'll also go for a hike on Sunday if the weather's nice. There's this trail I've been wanting to try.

Nice! Yeah, sometimes a chill weekend is exactly what you need. Recharge the batteries, you know?

Exactly! Alright, I should probably head back before someone wonders where I disappeared to. But hey, it was really good catching up!

Yeah, definitely! Good luck with the launch next week - you're going to crush it! And enjoy your weekend with your family!

Thanks so much! You enjoy your chill weekend too. And let me know what you think of The Bear!

Will do! Take care!

You too!
keywords: ready to get it out there
let me know how it is
culturalTips: Small talk é importante para construir relacionamentos
Demonstrar empatia e interesse pelos outros
